title: The Drovers
content:
آ آ آ آ Shrivelled leather, rusty buckles, and the rot is in our knuckles,
آ آ آ آ Scorched for months upon the pommel while the brittle rein hung free;
آ آ آ آ Shrunken eyes that once were lighted with fresh boyhood, dull and blighted,
آ آ آ آ And the sores upon our eyelids are unpleasant sights to see.
آ آ آ آ And our hair is thin and dying from the ends, with too long lying
آ آ آ آ In the night dews on the ashes of the Dry Countree.
آ آ آ آ Yes, weï؟½ve seen ï؟½em ï؟½bleaching whitelyï؟½ where the salt-bush sparkles brightly,
آ آ آ آ But their grins were over-friendly, so we passed and let them be.

آ آ آ آ And weï؟½ve seen them ï؟½rather recent,ï؟½ and weï؟½ve stopped to hide ï؟½em decent
آ آ آ آ When they werenï؟½t nice to handle and they werenï؟½t too nice to see;
آ آ آ آ We have heard the dry bones rattle under fifteen hundred cattle,
آ آ آ آ Seen the rags go up in dust-clouds and the brittle joints kicked free;
آ آ آ آ But thereï؟½s little time to tarry, if you wish to live and marry,
آ آ آ آ When the cattle shy at something in the Dry Countree.

آ آ آ آ No, you neednï؟½t fear the blacks on the Never Never tracks,
آ آ آ آ For the Myall in his freedomï؟½s an uncommon sight to see;
آ آ آ آ Oh! we do not stick at trifles, and the trackers sneak their rifles,
آ آ آ آ And go strolling in the gloaming while the sergeantï؟½s yarning free:
آ آ آ آ Round the Myalls creep the trackers, thereï؟½s a sound like firing crackers
آ آ آ آ And, the blacks are getting scarcer in the Dry Countree.
آ آ آ آ (Goes an unprotected maiden-ï؟½cross the clearing carrion-laden,
آ آ آ آ Oh they ride ï؟½em down on horseback in the Dry Countree.)

آ آ آ آ But you donï؟½t know what might happen when a tank is but a trap on
آ آ آ آ Roofs of hell, and there is nothing but the blaze of hell to see;
آ آ آ آ And the phantom waterï؟½s lapping, and no limb for saddle-strapping,
آ آ آ آ Better carry your revolver through the Dry Countree.
آ آ آ آ But Iï؟½m feeling gay and frisky, come with me and have a whisky!
آ آ آ آ Change of hells is all we live for (thatï؟½s my mate thatï؟½s got D.T.);
آ آ آ آ We have fought through hellï؟½s own weather, he and I and death together,
آ آ آ آ Oh, the devil grins to greet us from the Dry Countree!
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
